task_context.description: This is a particle detector design and performance evaluation task focused on creating a muon spectrometer capable of distinguishing between muons and pions. The task involves designing detector configurations with alternating absorber and tracking layers, then conducting comparative simulations to measure detection efficiency, particle stopping power, and energy deposition patterns. The user needs to test multiple detector geometries and absorber materials to determine the optimal configuration for muon identification while rejecting pion backgrounds. This is both a design exploration and a physics performance characterization study.
task_context.goal: Design and optimize a muon spectrometer for identifying muons and rejecting pions using absorber and tracking layers
constraints: Energy sweep capped at 50 GeV and 3 points maximum
Use particle energies of 5, 20, and 50 GeV
Absorber thickness should be 1-3 λ_I (not 8-12 λ_I like hadronic calorimeters)
Must simulate both muons (mu-) and pions (pi-) for comparison

observations.content: This workflow systematically explores three muon spectrometer configurations: iron absorber (baseline heavy material), aluminum absorber (lighter with less scattering), and tracking-only (no absorber baseline). Each configuration is tested with both muons and pions at 5, 20, and 50 GeV using energy sweeps for efficient parallel simulation. The analysis extracts key metrics including muon detection efficiency, pion stopping probability, and energy deposition profiles to determine the optimal configuration for muon identification while rejecting pion backgrounds.
Step completed successfully: Calculate optimal absorber thicknesses and layer configurations for three muon spectrometer designs based on physics principles

data.output:
{'report': '# Muon Spectrometer Design and Performance Evaluation Report

## Executive Summary

This report presents the design and performance evaluation of muon spectrometers for distinguishing between muons and pions. Three detector configurations were tested: an iron-based spectrometer, an aluminum-based spectrometer, and a tracking-only configuration. Each configuration was evaluated using particle simulations at three energy points (5, 20, and 50 GeV) for both muons (μ⁻) and pions (π⁻)

methodology_notes: Planned 13-step workflow: design_configurations, generate_iron_spectrometer, generate_aluminum_spectrometer, generate_tracking_only, simulate_muons_iron, simulate_pions_iron, simulate_muons_aluminum, simulate_pions_aluminum, simulate_muons_tracking, simulate_pions_tracking, analyze_performance, create_comparison_plots, generate_report
